questions.question: Explain the difference between Universal and Existential quantifiers with an example.
keyPoints: Universal quantifier (∀) states property holds for ALL elements in domain
Existential quantifier (∃) states property holds for AT LEAST ONE element
Example: ∀x Student(x) → Pass(x) vs ∃x Student(x) → Fail(x)

questions.question: What is Universal Instantiation? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: UI allows deriving ground instances from universally quantified statements
From ∀x P(x), infer P(c) for any constant c
Example: From ∀x Man(x) → Mortal(x), infer Man(Socrates) → Mortal(Socrates)

questions.question: Define the following FOL concepts: (i) Term (ii) Atomic Formula (iii) Well-Formed Formula
keyPoints: Term: constant, variable, or function application representing objects
Atomic Formula: predicate applied to terms, evaluates to true/false
Well-Formed Formula (wff): valid formula using connectives and quantifiers

questions.question: Explain Forward Chaining with its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Forward chaining is data-driven inference starting from known facts
Derives new facts using rules until goal is reached
Advantages: responds to new information, good for monitoring
Disadvantages: may generate irrelevant conclusions, less focused

questions.question: Convert the following English sentence to FOL: 'Every student has a mentor who is a professor'
keyPoints: ∀x (Student(x) → ∃y (Mentor(x,y) ∧ Professor(y)))
Alternative: ∃y (Professor(y) ∧ ∀x (Student(x) → Mentor(x,y)))
First version means each student has their own mentor, second means there is one professor who mentors all students

questions.question: What is Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens? Give one example each.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens: From P→Q and P, infer Q
Modus Tollens: From P→Q and ¬Q, infer ¬P
Example MP: If it rains(R) → wet(W), it rains, therefore wet
Example MT: If it rains → wet, not wet, therefore not rains

questions.question: What is a Horn clause? Explain its significance in logic programming.
keyPoints: Horn clause has at most one positive literal: (P∧Q∧...∧R)→S
Can be written as head :- body in Prolog
Efficient forward and backward chaining possible
Used in expert systems and logic programming languages

questions.question: Explain the Resolution inference rule in First-Order Logic. Describe the steps involved in resolution refutation and give an example proof.
keyPoints: Resolution is a refutation-complete inference rule for CNF
Two clauses with complementary literals can be resolved to produce new clause
Steps: Convert to CNF, negate goal, add to KB, resolve until empty clause
Example shows how contradiction proves query

questions.question: Describe the Knowledge Representation in FOL approach. How would you represent the following in FOL? (a) All birds can fly except penguins (b) Every person has exactly one biological mother
keyPoints: Use universal quantifiers with implications and biconditionals
(a) ∀x (Bird(x) ∧ ¬Penguin(x) → Flies(x)) OR ∀x (Bird(x) → (Penguin(x) ∨ Flies(x)))
(b) ∀x (Person(x) → ∃y (Mother(x,y) ∧ Person(y) ∧ ∀z (Mother(x,z) → z=y)))
Need uniqueness quantification for exactly one

questions.question: Explain Backward Chaining algorithm. How does it differ from Forward Chaining? Discuss situations where each is preferable.
keyPoints: Backward chaining is goal-driven, starts from query and works backwards
Uses goal reduction: to prove G, find rule G :- B1,...,Bn and prove B1,...,Bn
Differs from FC which is data-driven and works forward
Prefer BC when goal is specific, FC when data changes frequently or monitoring

questions.question: Define the following: (i) Sound inference (ii) Complete inference (iii) Unification (iv) Most General Unifier. Explain why these properties are important.
keyPoints: Sound: Only derives conclusions logically following from premises
Complete: Can derive any conclusion that follows from premises
Unification: Finding substitution making two literals identical
MGU: Most general substitution, least restrictive
Important for AI: ensure reasoning is correct (sound) and powerful (complete)
